summary: Jeff Atwood explores the UI design philosophy behind the game 'You Don't Know Jack,' which Jellyvision calls the Interactive Conversation Interface (iCi). The iCi approach falls between passive television (no user control) and unrestricted web navigation (complete user control), offering limited choices at any given moment. This creates a conversational dynamic where control is shared between designers and users, similar to human conversation where neither party has complete control. Atwood connects this to the familiar wizard UI pattern and Microsoft's Inductive User Interface, suggesting this guided-but-interactive approach would be significant in future OS design.
key_points: The Interactive Conversation Interface sits between passive media (TV) and fully interactive media (web), offering constrained choices at each moment
Shared control between program and user models real human conversation, where neither party can unilaterally dictate the discussion
The user influences but cannot completely determine their experience, while designers arrange all possible paths
This philosophy connects to the wizard UI metaphor and Microsoft's Inductive User Interface approach
money_quotes: You are not without influence over what you will experience, although you cannot completely decide what you will experience.
In a conversation, you can't unilaterally decide what gets discussed. The other person is not a machine.
The design team arranges for all the possible experiences. The individual's actions determine which experience actually transpires.
A television program gives you no options at all. The Web and multimedia programs usually allow you to go anywhere at any time you want. An iCi program falls between these extremes.
key_insight: The most engaging interactive experiences model human conversation by sharing control between designer and user, rather than offering either passive consumption or overwhelming freedom.
